planting.note: Scarify or cold-stratify seeds before fall direct sowing into sandy, dry soil in full sun.

directions: Silky Prairie Clover is a showstopping Great Plains native covered in silky white hairs that give the whole plant a silvery-gray sheen, topped in summer with dense cylindrical spikes of vivid rose-pink to magenta flowers with prominent orange anthers. Sow seeds directly in fall into loose, sandy, very well-drained, lean soil in full sun; seeds benefit from scarification or a brief cold-moist stratification period before planting. Once established, the deep root system makes this plant highly drought-tolerant and long-lived. It is an outstanding specialist bee plant rarely available commercially but invaluable in authentic Great Plains prairie restorations.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center - Dalea villosa

planting.note: Scarify seeds before fall direct sowing into dry, calcareous or rocky, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Roundhead Prairie Clover is a native perennial of dry rocky and calcareous prairies, limestone glades, and open hills from Kansas south to Texas and east to Alabama, distinguished from other prairie clovers by its rounded (rather than cylindrical) flower heads of small white blooms with striking orange anthers. Plant in dry, lean, well-drained calcareous or sandy soil in full sun; scarify seeds before fall direct sowing to improve germination rates. The finely divided, pinnate, gray-green foliage remains attractive even when the plant is not in bloom. It provides important nectar and pollen for specialist native bees of the Great Plains and south-central prairies and is very drought-tolerant once established.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center - Dalea multiflora

planting.note: Scarify seeds before fall direct sowing into very well-drained, dry, calcareous or sandy, lean soil in full sun.

directions: Compact Prairie Clover forms neat, low mounds of fine-textured, pinnate foliage studded in summer with dense, cylindrical spikes of bright purple to rose-violet flowers, making it one of the tidiest and most ornamental of the native prairie clovers for garden use. It is native to dry, rocky limestone glades and open sandy prairies of the south-central US and demands very well-drained, dry, calcareous or sandy, lean soil in full sun to perform well. Scarify seeds before fall direct sowing and avoid any soil that retains moisture, as this plant is prone to root rot in clay or poorly drained sites. Its compact habit makes it well-suited for the front edge of dry native prairie gardens, dry rock gardens, and limestone glade restorations where it also provides important forage for specialist Dalea bees.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center - Dalea compacta

planting.note: Scarify seeds before fall direct sowing into very well-drained, rocky, alkaline, calcareous soil in full sun.

directions: Dwarf Prairie Clover is one of the smallest Dalea species, forming low, spreading mats of woody prostrate stems clothed in tiny, resin-dotted pinnate leaves and producing small, dense spikes of bright pink to rose-purple flowers from spring through summer. Native to the dry Chihuahuan Desert and south Texas plains, it is among the most heat- and drought-adapted of the prairie clovers and must have extremely well-drained, rocky, alkaline, calcareous soil in full sun to thrive. Scarify seeds before fall sowing and avoid any supplemental irrigation once plants are established. Its prostrate, mat-forming habit makes it especially useful for dry rock gardens, xeriscape plantings, and rocky calcareous slope stabilization throughout zones 6-10.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center - Dalea nana

planting.note: Scarify seeds before fall direct sowing into dry, rocky, alkaline to neutral, calcareous or sandy, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Beardtip Prairie Clover is a native perennial of the Texas Hill Country and adjacent south-central states, where it grows on dry, rocky, calcareous hills and limestone-based prairies; its species name pogonathera means 'beard-bearing,' referring to the distinctive bearded tips of its petals that set it apart from other prairie clovers. It produces erect, branching stems with fine-textured pinnate foliage and dense, cylindrical spikes of bright rose-pink to magenta flowers in summer that attract specialist native Dalea bees, bumblebees, and hummingbirds. Grow in dry, rocky, alkaline to neutral, calcareous or sandy, very well-drained soil in full sun; scarify seeds before fall direct sowing and avoid supplemental watering once established. It is an outstanding drought-tolerant native for Texas Hill Country xeriscape gardens and limestone glade restorations.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center - Dalea pogonathera
